content.text: The cascade is CSS's algorithm for deciding which style declaration wins when multiple rules target the same element and property. Understanding it — and the related concepts of specificity, origin, inheritance, and source order — is what makes CSS predictable rather than mysterious.

attrs.alt: CSS Cascade Resolution Order — origin, @layer, specificity, source order

content.text: The Cascade Algorithm
When the browser styles an element, it collects all declarations that could apply to that element and property, then applies them in this order:
Origin and importance — which stylesheet the rule came from, and whether it carries !important
CSS @layer — if within the same origin, which cascade layer the rule belongs to
Specificity — how precisely the selector targets the element
Source order — if everything above ties, the later rule wins
These four steps are evaluated in sequence. If step 1 produces a clear winner, steps 2–4 are irrelevant.
Step 1 — Origin and Importance
CSS declarations come from three origins, each with a normal and an important tier. From lowest to highest precedence:
Tier
Origin
Description
1 (lowest)
User-agent (browser) — normal
Browser built-in defaults (headings are bold, links are blue)
User — normal
Reader's personal stylesheet (accessibility overrides, high contrast)
Author — normal
The site's own CSS files
Author — !important
Author rules with !important
User — !important
Reader's !important rules (e.g., for accessibility)
6 (highest)
User-agent — !important
Browser !important (rare)
Author !important beats normal author rules, but user !important still wins over author !important — ensuring accessibility user stylesheets can always override site CSS.
Step 2 — CSS @layer

Within the same origin, Cascade Layers (@layer) let you create ordered groups of styles. Rules in later-declared layers win over rules in earlier layers, regardless of specificity — as long as both rules are normal (no !important).
The order of @layer declarations determines priority — first declaration = lowest priority
Unlayered styles always beat all named @layer styles (they sit above all layers)
@layer is supported in all modern browsers since 2022
!important reverses the layer order — !important in an early layer beats !important in a late layer
Step 3 — Specificity
Specificity is a score assigned to each selector based on how precisely it identifies an element. It is represented as four values (a, b, c, d) compared left-to-right.
Selector part
Contributes to
Example
Score
Inline style attribute
a
style="color: red"
ID selector (#id)
b
#sidebar
Class selector (.cls)
c
.card
Attribute selector ([x])
c
[type="text"]
Pseudo-class (:hover)
c
:first-child
Type selector (div)
d
p
Pseudo-element (::before)
d
::placeholder
Universal selector (*)
Scores are combined for complex selectors:
Special rules:
:is(), :not(), :has() take the specificity of their most specific argument: :is(h1, .title) contributes 0,0,1,0
:where() always contributes zero specificity regardless of its arguments — useful for low-priority defaults
The universal selector (*) and combinators (space, >, +, ~) do not contribute to specificity
Specificity is not a single number — 0,1,0,0 always beats 0,0,100,0 (IDs beat any number of classes)
Step 4 — Source Order
When two declarations have the same origin, layer, and specificity, the one that appears later in the document wins.
Source order also determines which stylesheet wins when two separate files have the same specificity — the file linked later in HTML wins:

<link rel="stylesheet" href="theme.css">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="overrides.css">  <!-- wins when specificity ties -->
Inheritance

Properties that are not explicitly set on an element may be inherited from their parent. This is different from the cascade — it is a fallback that only applies when no rule sets the property directly.
Inherited (propagate to children)
Not inherited (each element gets initial value)
color
margin, padding
font-family, font-size, font-weight, font-style
border, outline
line-height, letter-spacing
background
text-align, text-decoration
width, height
list-style, list-style-type
display, position
cursor
box-shadow, transform
visibility
overflow
Inheritance keywords let you control inheritance explicitly:
CSS Custom Properties and the Cascade
CSS custom properties (also called CSS variables) participate in the cascade and inheritance just like any property. They are declared with -- prefix and read with var():
Custom properties cascade like any property — a more specific rule can override a variable
Custom properties inherit — a variable set on :root is available to all elements
Custom properties are resolved at computed-value time — they can reference other variables
Undefined or invalid var() resolves to the property's initial value if no fallback is provided
The !important Override
Adding !important to a declaration moves it to the author-important tier (step 1) and overrides all normal declarations, regardless of specificity.
When two declarations both carry !important, specificity determines the winner — the rules are evaluated normally but within the author-important tier.
When !important is useful
When !important is harmful
Overriding inline styles in third-party widgets
As a shortcut to fix specificity problems
Utility classes that must always apply (display:none)
In component CSS — creates unmaintainable debt
Accessibility overrides (user stylesheets)
To beat another !important — creates an arms race
!important breaks the cascade. Reach for @layer or higher-specificity selectors before using it.
Angular: ViewEncapsulation and the Cascade

Angular's default ViewEncapsulation.Emulated works by adding a unique attribute to both the component's HTML and its CSS selectors. This transforms all component selectors into attribute selectors, which have higher specificity (adding 0,0,1,0) than bare class selectors.
This has cascade implications when global styles try to override component styles:
To override a component's styles from a global stylesheet, you need either higher specificity or the Angular-specific :host combinator and global styles scope:
The CSS custom property approach (option 3) is the cleanest — it creates a documented theming API for the component without breaking encapsulation.
Debugging the Cascade in DevTools

Chrome and Firefox DevTools both show which declarations are active and which are overridden (struck through) in the Styles panel. The Computed tab shows the final computed value and which rule set it.
Struck-through rules in the Styles panel are overridden — they lost the cascade
Hover over a property value to see the property's specificity score
The Computed panel lets you trace back which source rule set each computed value
Use the selector filter in the Styles panel to find rules matching a specific selector text
Common Cascade Pitfalls
Specificity creep: fixing overrides by adding IDs or !important instead of restructuring selectors
Unexpected inheritance: child elements inheriting a property you meant only for the parent — use initial or a scoped selector
Global styles vs encapsulated Angular components: forgetting that ViewEncapsulation.Emulated raises specificity
Property is set but inherited value shows: a more specific scoped rule is resetting it — check the Computed panel
@layer order reversed: adding a layer after defining styles in it — the last @layer declaration is highest priority
Custom property fallback confusion: var(--x, red) does not fall back if --x is defined as empty string — it falls back on undefined or invalid
CSS Reset vs Normalize — Managing User-Agent Styles
Browser user-agent stylesheets differ subtly between browsers (margins on headings, padding on lists, font sizes). Two common strategies deal with this:
Strategy
What it does
Common library
When to use
CSS Reset
Removes all browser defaults — zero margins, no bullets, no heading sizes
Eric Meyer Reset, CSS Reset 2.0
When you build every style from scratch (design systems)
Normalize.css
Preserves useful browser defaults, fixes cross-browser inconsistencies
normalize.css, modern-normalize
When you want a consistent baseline, not blank-slate
Custom baseline
Targeted resets for box-sizing, images, fonts only
none — hand-written
Modern Angular apps — precise control without full reset overhead
A minimal modern baseline that works well with Angular:
Worked Example — Tracing a Cascade Conflict
Debugging cascade conflicts is a core CSS skill. Here is a complete trace through all four cascade steps for a real scenario:
Home
Resolving which color applies to the <a> element:
Origin: all rules are normal author styles — all in the same tier
@layer: reset.css and styles.css are in named layers; nav.css is unlayered. Unlayered wins — nav.css rules are candidates
Specificity of unlayered candidates: #site-nav a = 0,1,0,1; .nav-link = 0,0,1,1; .nav-link.active = 0,0,2,1. ID selector wins: #site-nav a → color: #1e3a5f
BUT the inline style (style="color: white") has specificity 1,0,0,0 which beats all stylesheet rules. Winner: color: white
If you remove the inline style, the winner becomes #site-nav a { color: #1e3a5f } because an ID selector beats any number of classes.
Strategies for Manageable CSS Architecture
Knowing the cascade lets you architect CSS that does not spiral into specificity wars. The most common patterns:
BEM — Block Element Modifier
BEM avoids specificity issues by using only class selectors — never nesting or using IDs — which keeps all rules at 0,0,1,0 specificity. Conflicts are resolved by source order alone:
Utility-First (Tailwind-style)
Utility-first CSS uses single-purpose classes with the same low specificity. Conflicts are resolved by layer order (Tailwind v4 uses @layer utilities) and the HTML order classes are applied:

<!-- Utilities apply in source order — latter utility wins on conflict -->
<div class="p-4 p-8">  <!-- p-8 wins because it comes later in the stylesheet -->
@layer Architecture
The recommended modern approach for large Angular applications — declare all layers upfront so the priority order is explicit:
Inheritance Deep Dive — with and without explicit values
Inheritance applies when no cascade rule sets a property. Understanding when it activates versus when it does not lets you write more precise CSS:
The all shorthand property is a useful escape hatch for resetting everything:
all: revert is the safest reset for web components or third-party widgets — it falls back to browser defaults, not blank
all: initial is more aggressive — it removes even font-family from headings
all: unset is useful for clearing form element styles while retaining font inheritance
Individual property resets are usually better than all: — they are more targeted and less surprising
Testing the Cascade — Practical Exercises
Build intuition for the cascade by trying these in a CodePen or the browser DevTools:
Create a paragraph with three rules: type (p), class (.intro), and ID (#first). Add text to each. Change source order to see how it breaks the tie.
Add !important to the type selector. Notice it now beats the ID selector in normal specificity but loses to a user important rule.

Declare @layer low, high; and put the p rule in @layer low and the .intro rule in @layer high. Note that even though .intro has higher specificity, the layer order would have mattered if they were equal.
Set color on a <div> and notice that nested <span> inherits it without a rule. Then set color: initial on the <span> and notice it reverts to black (the spec initial value), not the browser default.
Summary

The cascade resolves style conflicts through four steps: origin and importance (which stylesheet and whether !important is present), CSS @layer order, specificity (inline > ID > class/attr/pseudo-class > type), and source order as a final tiebreaker. Inheritance provides a separate fallback for properties not explicitly set. CSS custom properties participate in both the cascade and inheritance, making them the preferred mechanism for theming and component APIs. In Angular, ViewEncapsulation.Emulated adds attribute selectors that raise component style specificity — plan for this when writing global override styles.
